title: Why watch Eva
wikitext:
[]: Why watch Eva?             Quote Post by Hathslippy » Mon Mar 30, 2015 11:39 am I have a friend, who does not want to watch Eva for no real reason: Convincing reasons why he should watch it (I need to show him the replies from other people that aren't me)

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Mon Mar 30, 2015 11:55 am I don't think you should force him to watch it. If he's not interested, he won't get anything out of it. That is, ironically, the best reason I can come up with.

[]:             Quote Post by Gendo'sPapa » Mon Mar 30, 2015 2:32 pm Just talk to your friend about why YOU love Eva. That's really the only way to get a friend to watch it on their own accord. Try to express what it is about the show that you love & if you're successful then you'll spark an interest in them. But don't try to force them. If they don't want to watch Eva then that's a perfectly valid excuse for them not to watch it. Just look around the forum. There's enough discussion here to at least show Eva has it's worth.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Mar 30, 2015 3:37 pm The 1995 series is a classic which transformed anime in many ways, both obvious and subtle, still getting shout-outs in other anime, and becoming part of wider Japanese pop-culture. You watch it to see why there might be horse-racing promotions with the Evangelion brand, and why there's a reference to a series called Avantgardian in the recently finished Shirobako anime. OTOH, even staff members from the production are on record as saying that it isn't aimed at normal well-adjusted people.

[]:             Quote Post by BC Baron » Mon Mar 30, 2015 5:37 pm Is your friend interested in watching any other anime shows? If so, which ones? Getting a better handle on his overall preferences might provide a better starting point. Before watching NGE, I was already fairly familiar with anime in general, so there wasn't too much of a culture shock. I grew up enjoying titles from the space opera and mecha genres, so the setting for Eva already felt somewhat familiar. If your friend doesn't have that kind of background, he might not be drawn toward the series in the same way I was. Hope that helps a little.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Mon Mar 30, 2015 5:46 pm BC Baron wrote: Before watching NGE, I was already fairly familiar with anime in general, so there wasn't too much of a culture shock.

[]:             Quote Post by BC Baron » Mon Mar 30, 2015 7:14 pm ^ I don't think it's a prerequisite to be a fan of mecha shows in order to find something to appreciate about NGE, but it certainly doesn't hurt.

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Mon Mar 30, 2015 7:16 pm ^ I think the second half works even better when you've seen and loved some of the mech shows released prior to Eva.

[]:             Quote Post by Compiling_Autumn » Mon Mar 30, 2015 9:01 pm Evangelion isn't the only anime that matters, but it's the one that matters most. No show has knocked me on my ass the way EVA has. It's probably one of the most "penetrating" shows you can watch w/r/t what it has to say about the way modern day people live.

[]:             Quote Post by Hathslippy » Tue Mar 31, 2015 4:51 pm BC Baron wrote: Is your friend interested in watching any other anime shows? If so, which ones?

[]:             Quote Post by IronEvangelion » Tue Mar 31, 2015 11:58 pm If he likes romance, you might want to tell him about the relationships between Shinji, Rei, and Asuka, as well as how the show is basically about the barriers between people that prevent us from forming relationships with each other, and the cast is trying to overcome those barriers. Since he complains about animation, make sure to warn him about the drop in animation quality in the later episodes.

[]:             Quote Post by BC Baron » Wed Apr 01, 2015 1:00 am @Hathslippy: Unfortunately, I don't think there's an abundance of romance to work with in NGE (or at least not in the mushy, Valentines Day sense of the term). Although I would argue that Misato & Kaji have their moments and even Touji & Hikari manage to find themselves in one scene that I found somewhat heartwarming. For an introduction to mecha with a side order of romance in the form of love triangles, you might want to consider starting him out on something like Macross or even Nadesico.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Apr 01, 2015 1:11 am BC Baron wrote: I don't think there's an abundance of romance to work with in NGE (or at least not in the mushy, Valentines Day sense of the term).

[]:             Quote Post by Tumbling Down » Fri Apr 03, 2015 8:44 pm I'd say "Watch Evangelion if you want insight into social anxiety, depression, and their interconnection, but in the palatable form of a giant fighting robot show."

[]:             Quote Post by LordThaeon » Tue Apr 14, 2015 3:24 am Why watch Eva? For the same reason that sociology researchers and psychologists still review Freud even despite the heavy flaws in his methodology and the findings of his theories. Evangelion was an anime that reminded it's audience that the people in the giant robot scene were all human with their own doubts, flaws, hopes, desires, dreams, strengths and weaknesses. It deconstructed the mecha genre in ways that continue to influence later anime even to this present day. Even if latter anime either disproved Eva's thesis/conclusions or countered them, there's no doubt that evangelion still left a clear impression. The show's depiction of the characters and their personal/psychological issues was an edge that was seldom explored as closely as Evangelion did at that time. Not to mention it's exploration of a main protagonist who was constantly at war with himself on a mental/psychological/spiritual level for the simple fact that he saw himself as weak and didn't know how to resolve that. Which stands in sharp contrast to most self-confident anime protagonists who stomp their way through the story. (especially at the time) It's also simply a good anime to watch. Though I'd give your friend fair warning about the original ending and EOE.

[]:             Quote Post by Justacrazyguy » Tue Apr 14, 2015 5:29 am LordThaeon wrote: Even if latter anime either disproved Eva's thesis/conclusions or countered them,

[]:             Quote Post by LordThaeon » Tue Apr 14, 2015 6:22 am Justacrazyguy wrote: ? How do you counter "Even if life/love/relationships with other people hurts, keep living/loving"? Or are you talking about another theme of the show? The central message looks very hard to disprove.

[]:             Quote Post by Compiling_Autumn » Tue Apr 14, 2015 8:39 am LordThaeon wrote: Perhaps disprove was the wrong word to use. What I should've said is that later series expanded on the thesis started by evangelion and also improved on it by filling in the holes that evangelions methodology and conclusions left. How the themes of Eva are portrayed and the methods which the story goes through to justify its own themes are most akin to a coin with one side being covered in mud and that's the only side that's ever shown. Not to mention the several artificial contrivances which the plot pulls just to justify itself and force its characters to continually walk around in metaphorical circles. That said, I'd still recommend for people to watch it. Both because the anime is good and because it can be learned from in the same way that psychologists still learn from Freud.

[]:             Quote Post by LordThaeon » Tue Apr 14, 2015 4:58 pm Compiling_Autumn wrote: Which anime shows would you say expand on NGE's themes in this way?

[]:             Quote Post by Seven Fifty » Wed Apr 15, 2015 5:59 pm You could just tell him it's a brilliant piece of television. Emphasis, possibly, on television - merely saying it's a brilliant anime may not be enough if your friend doesn't watch anime, or thinks that all the best TV is non-anime.
summary:
[]: Why watch Eva?             Quote Post by Hathslippy » Mon Mar 30, 2015 11:39 am I have a friend, who does not want to watch Eva for no real reason: Convincing reasons why he should watch it (I need to show him the replies from other people that aren't me)

[]:             Quote Post by robersora » Mon Mar 30, 2015 11:55 am I don't think you should force him to watch
